question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/properties/TraceProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: contractFilterCheck()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that contractFilterCheck() performs a database lookup via commonEntityAccessor.get() for every EVM opcode execution when tracing is enabled, by submitting high-volume contract calls that generate thousands of entity lookups per request, exhausting database connection pools and causing 30%+ of nodes to fail to process further requests?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/properties/TraceProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: stateFilterCheck()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the per-opcode invocation of stateFilterCheck() to cause a thundering herd of database queries via commonEntityAccessor.get() (called after stateFilterCheck() passes), by submitting concurrent requests that each execute thousands of opcodes, exhausting the DB connection pool and causing 30%+ of nodes to become unresponsive?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/properties/TraceProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: contractFilterCheck()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the @Hex validation constraint on the contract set only validates at configuration binding time (not at runtime in contractFilterCheck()), allowing a runtime bypass where a non-hex string could be inserted into the contract set via a Spring config refresh, causing contractFilterCheck() to behave unexpectedly and either crash or trace all contracts?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/properties/TraceProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: stateFilterCheck()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the enabled flag in TraceProperties is a simple boolean with no atomic update guarantee, causing a TOCTOU race where tracing is checked as disabled but then enabled between the isEnabled() check and the stateFilterCheck() call, leading to inconsistent tracing behavior that could cause unexpected log floods on 30%+ of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/properties/TraceProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: contractFilterCheck()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the open-by-default contract filter (empty set = trace all) to cause a log amplification attack where a single eth_call to a contract with a complex internal call graph generates hundreds of log entries (one per opcode per call frame), causing log write throughput to exceed disk write speed and crash 30%+ of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/properties/TraceProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: stateFilterCheck()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the interaction between the enabled flag and stateFilterCheck() to cause a log injection attack by embedding ANSI escape codes or terminal control sequences in EVM input data, which are then logged verbatim via log.info(), corrupting terminal-based log monitoring tools on 30%+ of nodes and causing operators to miss critical alerts?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/properties/TraceProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: contractFilterCheck()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that contractFilterCheck() is invoked synchronously in the EVM execution hot path (tracePostExecution), by crafting contract calls that cause the HashSet.contains() lookup to degrade to O(n) due to hash collisions in the contract address strings, causing CPU exhaustion on 30%+ of nodes under concurrent load?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/properties/TraceProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: stateFilterCheck()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that stateFilterCheck() is called in the synchronous EVM execution path to cause a thread starvation attack, by submitting a flood of requests that each trigger thousands of stateFilterCheck() calls and subsequent log.info() calls, blocking EVM execution threads and causing 30%+ of nodes to stop processing new requests?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/properties/TraceProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: contractFilterCheck()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any circuit breaker or backpressure mechanism in the tracing path triggered by contractFilterCheck() returning false, to cause a sustained log write storm that fills the filesystem on 30%+ of nodes, preventing the JVM from writing GC logs or other critical files and causing JVM crashes?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/properties/TraceProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: stateFilterCheck()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the status Set<State> in TraceProperties is initialized as a mutable HashSet, and that Spring's @ConfigurationProperties binding may replace the set reference during a live reload, causing stateFilterCheck() to read from a stale or partially-populated set and either trace too much (disk exhaustion) or too little (missed filtering) on 30%+ of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/properties/TraceProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: contractFilterCheck()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the contract set in TraceProperties is a mutable HashSet that is directly returned by getContract(), allowing any code with a reference to the TraceProperties bean to modify the set and bypass contractFilterCheck() filtering, causing unintended trace logging that floods disk on 30%+ of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/properties/TraceProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: stateFilterCheck()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the status set is directly returned by getStatus() (mutable reference via @Data-generated getter), allowing any Spring bean that injects TraceProperties to clear or modify the status set, causing stateFilterCheck() to always return false and trace all states, flooding logs on 30%+ of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/properties/TraceProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: contractFilterCheck()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the log.info() call triggered when contractFilterCheck() returns false to cause a log4j/logback appender to perform expensive operations (e.g., remote syslog, JDBC appender) for each opcode execution, causing network saturation or database overload on 30%+ of nodes when tracing is enabled?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/properties/TraceProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: stateFilterCheck()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that stateFilterCheck() has no caching or memoization, causing repeated HashSet.isEmpty() and HashSet.contains() calls for every EVM opcode in a high-gas transaction, creating measurable CPU overhead that degrades throughput on 30%+ of nodes under concurrent load?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/properties/TraceProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: contractFilterCheck()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that contractFilterCheck() is called after a database lookup (commonEntityAccessor.get()) for every opcode execution, by crafting contract calls that target non-existent contract addresses, causing the entity lookup to always return Optional.empty() and skip the filter check entirely, ensuring all opcodes are traced and flooding logs on 30%+ of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/properties/TraceProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: stateFilterCheck()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the interaction between the enabled flag and stateFilterCheck() to cause a log amplification attack by submitting eth_estimateGas requests (which execute the EVM multiple times in a binary search loop), causing each estimation attempt to generate a full trace log, multiplying log volume by the number of estimation iterations on 30%+ of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/properties/TraceProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: contractFilterCheck()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the @Hex annotation validates contract addresses at configuration binding time but contractFilterCheck() performs a plain string comparison at runtime, by crafting a scenario where the hex string representation of a contract address differs between the configured filter and the runtime-computed value (e.g., leading zeros, checksum casing), causing the filter to always miss and trace all contracts?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/properties/TraceProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: stateFilterCheck()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the State enum values used in stateFilterCheck() are deserialized from configuration strings, by providing an invalid State value in the hiero.mirror.web3.evm.trace.status property, causing a BindException during application startup or config refresh that prevents 30%+ of nodes from starting or reloading configuration?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/properties/TraceProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: contractFilterCheck()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the contract set in TraceProperties is a HashSet (unordered, non-deterministic iteration order) to cause non-deterministic tracing behavior across different nodes, where the same contract call is traced on some nodes but not others, leading to inconsistent state across 30%+ of nodes and potential consensus failures?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/properties/TraceProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: stateFilterCheck()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the tracing path triggered by stateFilterCheck() returning false logs the full input data (frame.getInputData().toShortHexString()), and that toShortHexString() may still produce very long strings for large inputs, causing string allocation pressure in the JVM that triggers frequent GC pauses and degrades throughput on 30%+ of nodes?
